skill.name: zach-feature-demand-validator
skill.description:
功能需求真伪验证器。用三维数据（Review/关键词/社区）验证微创新是否真实需求。
使用时机：品类选定后评估微创新、竞品分析发现差异点后判断要不要跟进。
触发词：/zach-feature-demand-validator
skill.summary: Validate Amazon micro-innovation feature demand using three-dimensional data: reviews, keywords, and community signals.
skill.author: zach22-1999

security_audit.summary: The skill is a legitimate Amazon product research and demand validation tool. All 395 static findings are false positives: the 354 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' hits are markdown inline code formatting (backticks in documentation), the 'PowerShell invocation' hits are WINDOWS_USAGE.md usage examples, the 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' and 'Certificate/key files' hits are keyword matches on words like 'validate', 'cert', 'key' in legitimate code, and the high-entropy heuristic matches are on YAML frontmatter delimiters. The Python scripts are standard CSV parsing utilities with no malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: External command references in documentation
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged 354 instances of 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' across markdown files. These are all false positives: backticks in markdown are inline code formatting (e.g., `python script.py`), not actual command execution. The SKILL.md, README.md, and reference files contain usage examples and documentation in markdown format.

low_findings.title: PowerShell references in WINDOWS_USAGE.md
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged PowerShell invocations in scripts/WINDOWS_USAGE.md. These are documentation examples showing Windows users how to run the Python scripts, not actual executable PowerShell payloads.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs in documentation and manifest
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged hardcoded URLs in source_manifest.json and reference files. These are example Amazon product URLs used for documentation purposes, not credential exfiltration endpoints.

content.user_title: Validate Amazon Feature Demand with 3D Data Analysis
content.value_statement: Amazon sellers often add micro-innovations based on assumption rather than real consumer demand. This skill validates whether a feature idea is a genuine user need using three independent data dimensions: review signals, keyword signals, and community signals.
actual_capabilities: Parse Sorftime product_reviews JSON exports and filter feature-related reviews by keyword matching
Extract demand signals from Amazon reviews using configurable feature and moisture keyword lists
Generate keyword CSV exports from Sorftime keyword_detail, keyword_trend, and keyword_extends data
Parse user-provided review_source_pack directories containing CSV, TXT, or HTML review exports
Validate deliverable report directories for completeness (CSV files, report markdown, data files)
Cross-reference review signals, keyword trends, and community discussions (Reddit, Quora) to assess real demand
limitations: Requires Sorftime MCP access for the optimal data pipeline, though fallback paths via WebSearch/WebFetch exist
Keyword analysis depends on category-specific keyword construction strategies documented in references
Community signal gathering is limited to publicly searchable content (Reddit, Quora) and cannot access private forums
Does not predict future demand trends, only validates existing signals at the time of analysis

use_cases.title: Validate micro-innovation before tooling investment
use_cases.description: An Amazon seller has identified a product category and wants to add a small feature improvement. Use this skill to check if real consumers are actually requesting that feature in reviews and search queries before committing to mold and manufacturing costs.
use_cases.target_user: Amazon product developers and private label sellers

use_cases.title: Competitive differentiation analysis
use_cases.description: After completing competitor deep-dive research, a seller finds feature differences between competitors. This skill determines whether those differences represent genuine market demand or just competitor design choices that consumers do not actually care about.
use_cases.target_user: E-commerce product managers and category analysts

use_cases.title: New product listing optimization
use_cases.description: Before writing a new product listing, validate which features and keywords should be emphasized based on actual review complaints and search behavior data, ensuring the listing addresses real consumer needs.
use_cases.target_user: Amazon listing copywriters and SEO specialists

prompt_templates.title: Basic feature demand check
prompt_templates.prompt: Use /zach-feature-demand-validator to check if 'wireless charging' is a real demand for desk lamps in the US market. Read references/judgment_criteria.md first to understand the three-dimensional scoring.
prompt_templates.scenario: First-time user wanting a simple yes/no validation on a single feature

prompt_templates.title: Full three-dimensional validation
prompt_templates.prompt: Validate the micro-innovation of adding a built-in steamer to garment steamers. Run the full three-dimensional analysis: parse Sorftime reviews for steamer-related complaints, analyze keyword trends for 'built-in steamer', and search Reddit for consumer discussions about this feature gap.
prompt_templates.scenario: Product developer needing comprehensive validation before investment decision

prompt_templates.title: Fallback pipeline without Sorftime
prompt_templates.prompt: I do not have Sorftime access. Use the WebSearch and WebFetch fallback pipeline to gather review signals from Amazon product pages and community signals from Reddit. Parse my local review_source_pack at /path/to/pack for the review dimension.
prompt_templates.scenario: User without Sorftime MCP access needing alternative data sources

prompt_templates.title: Competitive gap validation
prompt_templates.prompt: I found that Competitor A has an LED display on their USB cable but Competitor B does not. Before I copy this feature, validate whether 'USB cable with wattage display' is a genuine consumer demand or just a design choice. Use the three-dimensional method to score this.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced user doing competitive analysis and feature copying decisions

output_examples.input: Validate if 'wireless charging' is a real demand for desk lamps based on review complaints and keyword trends
output: Feature: wireless charging on desk lamps
Review dimension: 47 complaints about cable clutter in desk lamp reviews (12% of negative reviews)
Keyword dimension: 'desk lamp wireless charger' shows 8,200 monthly searches, growing 15% YoY
Community dimension: 23 Reddit threads in r/productivity asking for wireless charging desk lamps
Verdict: Pursue — genuine unmet need confirmed across all three dimensions

output_examples.input: Validate 'built-in steamer' feature for garment steamer products using Sorftime data
output: Review dimension: 89 reviews mention 'separate steamer' as pain point, 34 mention 'wish it had steamer'
Keyword dimension: 'garment steamer with attachment' — 2,100 searches, stable volume
Community dimension: Low Reddit/Quora signal (6 threads), mostly questions not demands
Verdict: Skip — keyword and community signals too weak despite review complaints

best_practices: Always read references/judgment_criteria.md before starting validation to understand the three-dimensional scoring methodology
Use the parse_reviews.py script with Sorftime JSON exports for the most reliable review signal extraction
Cross-validate findings across all three dimensions — strong demand should appear in reviews, keywords, and community discussions simultaneously
Export deliverables using the validate_deliverables.py script to ensure report completeness before delivery
anti_patterns: Do not skip the keyword dimension and rely only on review analysis — review complaints alone can be misleading about actual purchase intent
Do not use placeholder ASIN values like MULTI, UNKNOWN, or B0XXXXX — the validation script will reject them
Do not conclude demand exists from a single dimension — all three signals (review, keyword, community) must show alignment
Do not ignore the moisture keywords filter — it removes irrelevant matches like 'damp' in non-moisture contexts

faq.question: What if I do not have Sorftime MCP access?
faq.answer: The skill includes fallback paths. Use WebSearch and WebFetch to gather review and community signals, and prepare a local review_source_pack directory with CSV or HTML review exports. The parse_review_source_pack.py script handles user-provided data.

faq.question: How does the three-dimensional scoring work?
faq.answer: The skill cross-validates demand across three independent data sources: review complaints (what users complain about), keyword search volume (what users actively search for), and community discussions (what users ask about on Reddit and Quora). Each dimension contributes to a final 0-100 score.

faq.question: Can I validate multiple features at once?
faq.answer: Yes. Run the validation flow once per feature. Each feature gets its own three-dimensional score and verdict. You can compare scores across features to prioritize your product development roadmap.

faq.question: What file formats are supported for review_source_pack?
faq.answer: The parse_review_source_pack.py script supports CSV (with flexible column aliases), plain text (TXT/MD), and HTML review exports. The source_manifest.json must specify the ASIN, site, and capture timestamp.

faq.question: How accurate is the demand prediction?
faq.answer: The skill validates existing demand signals, not future trends. Accuracy depends on data freshness — recent Sorftime exports and current community discussions produce more reliable results than stale data.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to run on my system?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill only reads Amazon review data, keyword APIs (Sorftime), and public web content. It writes CSV report files to your local output directory. No system-level operations or network exfiltration occur.
